text.title: SERP Alert Rules — Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules every SERP alerting setup must satisfy. Skipping any one produces either alarm fatigue or the dreaded six-day-late discovery of a traffic collapse.

rule.statement: Alerting MUST evaluate four signals nightly: (a) impressions week-over-week delta for tracked queries, (b) position delta for tracked queries, (c) AI Overview presence/absence on tracked queries, (d) CTR delta on stable impressions. Missing any signal leaves a blind spot.

rationale.source: Search Console API documentation; post-2024 SERP feature literature

rule.rationale: Impressions catch broad visibility changes. Position catches algorithm hits per query. AI Overview catches the new dominant feature shift. CTR catches snippet / title changes. A monitor that only watches impressions misses the AI Overview that just appeared on the head query.

rule.statement: An alert fires only if the threshold is breached for ≥2 consecutive days (or ≥7 days for weekly seasonality). Single-day spikes do not fire — they often reflect Google data-pipeline noise.

rationale.source: Search Console data-pipeline behaviour; rank-tracker SLA variability

rule.rationale: Search Console reporting lags 1-3 days and sometimes back-fills. A single-day -25% drop is often a partial-data artefact that resolves the next day. The sustained-window rule eliminates the most common false positive.

rule.statement: AI Overview presence on a tracked query is its own alert class (not folded into position) AND its appearance/disappearance triggers an investigation regardless of whether traffic changed yet.

rationale.source: post-2024 SERP feature observations; AI Overview adoption literature

rule.rationale: AI Overviews change CTR before they change impressions and may compress positions silently. The alert must fire on the structural change, not wait for downstream traffic damage.

rule.statement: Tracked queries are tagged by priority band (head = revenue-driving, body = supporting, long-tail = exploration). Alerts on head queries route to immediate-review; body to daily digest; long-tail to weekly summary.
rule.rationale: Treating every query equally produces digest sprawl. Head queries deserve interruption; long-tail movement is informational. The priority band is the difference between a usable inbox and an ignored one.

rule.statement: Alerts fire only Mon-Fri 09:00-19:00 operator local time unless the affected query is critical (head + revenue path). Saturday-night SERP volatility is real but rarely actionable in real time.
rule.rationale: A Saturday alert on a long-tail query is noise. The operator cannot publish content fixes over the weekend in most agencies. Hard quiet-hours protect attention; the Monday digest covers the gap.

list.item: ai_overview_appeared: any tracked query — always fires
ai_overview_disappeared: any tracked query — always fires

example.title: AI Overview appearance triggers an investigation
example.code: Tuesday 03:00 — nightly pull. Query "best CRM for freelancers" (head, priority 1) has new AI Overview snippet citing 3 competitors, not us. Tuesday 09:00 — alert fires; investigation log opened. Tuesday 11:00 — content team identifies our page lacks the structured comparison section the AI Overview pulls from. Update drafted. Wednesday — update published. Friday — AI Overview now cites us; CTR partially recovers.
example.note: r3 (AI Overview as first-class signal), r4 (head priority), r5 (work hours). Caught within 6 hours of the SERP change.

example.title: The weekly-review-only failure
example.code: Monday weekly review: impressions down 32% from previous week. Investigation: AI Overview appeared Thursday last week and has been showing competitors for 5 days. By the time the fix ships, recovery takes 3 weeks instead of 1.
example.note: r1, r3 violated. Weekly review is too slow for AI Overview shifts.
